title: Named Registers
subtitle: "a–"z — your 26 personal slots. Capitals append.
summary: Lowercase "a–"z are 26 named registers you write to explicitly. Uppercase "A–"Z append to the same registers instead of overwriting.

blocks.text: 26 lowercase letters give you 26 named registers. Pick one with {key:"}{letter} *before* an operator: {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:y} yanks the line *into register a*. {key:"}{key:a}{key:p} pastes it back.

blocks.label: Append yanked line to register a

blocks.title: Capitals Append
blocks.text:
{key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:y} **overwrites** register `a` with the current line.

{key:"}{key:A}{key:y}{key:y} **appends** the current line to register `a` (preserving what was already there).

This is one of Vim's small genius moves: collecting a list of items into one register without an external paste buffer.

Classic use: scan a file, find every line matching `TODO`, append each to register `t`, then dump them somewhere with {key:"}{key:t}{key:p}.

With {key::}{key:g}{key:/}{pat}{key:/}{key:y} {key:T}, the global command does the same thing: yank each matching line *appending* to register `t`. Then put.

blocks.title: Same Registers Hold Macros
blocks.text:
When you record a macro with {key:q}{key:a}…{key:q}, those keystrokes go into **register `a`** — exactly the same `a` you yank text into. Macros are just register contents.

This means you can:

  • Yank a sequence of keystrokes you typed into a buffer with {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}, then play it as a macro with {key:@}{key:a}.
  • Edit a macro by pasting it into the buffer ({key:"}{key:a}{key:p}), tweaking, then yanking back ({key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:$}). See [`macros.editing`](#macros.editing).
  • Compose macros: {key:"}{key:A}{key:y}{key:y} appends a line to a recorded macro register, extending what {key:@}{key:a} will replay.

This unification is one of the most beautiful things in Vim's design.

blocks.title: Named registers are explicit destinations
blocks.text: Selecting a register with {key:"}{key:a} routes the next command's output (or input, for paste) through that slot — once. The selection is consumed by the next operator, then resets. You can't 'set' a register persistently in normal mode; you can only direct individual commands at one. Capital letters ({key:"}{key:A}) append instead of overwrite.
